concepts.title: Carnbonate ion
concepts.conceptBody:
Qualitative Analysis

Classification of Anions

Methods available for the detection of anions are not as systematic as those used for the detection of cations. Furthermore anions are classified essentially on the basis of process employed.

Class A: Includes anions that are identified by volatile products obtained on treatment with acids. It is further divided into two sub groups.

(i) Gases evolved with dil. $\text{HCl}/$ dil ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

(ii) Gases or acid vapours evolved with conc. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$

Class B: Includes anions that are identified by their reactions in solution.

Class A: (i) Anions which evolve gases on reaction with dil. $\text{HCl}/$ dil. ${{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

1. Carbonate $\mathbf{CO}_{\mathbf{3}}^{\mathbf{2-}}$

(i) Dilute $\text{HCl}$: It gives effervescence, due to the evolution of carbon dioxide

$\text{CO}_{3}^{2-}+2{{\text{H}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow +{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

The gas gives white turbidity with lime water and baryta water.

$\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}+2\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow +{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+\text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}+2\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{BaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow +{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

On prolonged passage of carbon dioxide in lime water, the turbidity slowly disappears due to the formation of soluble hydrogen carbonate of calcium.

$\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow +\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{Ca}{{\left( \text{HC}{{\text{O}}_{3}} \right)}_{2}}$

The following tests performed with then aqueous salts solution.

(ii) Barium chloride or Calcium chloride solution: White ppt. of barium or calcium carbonate is obtained, which is soluble in mineral acid.

$\text{CO}_{3}^{2-}+\text{B}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{BaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow $

$\text{CO}_{3}^{2-}+\text{C}{{\text{a}}^{2+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{CaC}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow $

(iii) Silver nitrate solution: White ppt. of silver carbonate is obtained.

$\text{CO}_{3}^{2-}+2\text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow $

The ppt. so obtained is soluble in nitric acid and in ammonia, the ppt. becomes yellow or brown on addition of excess reagent and same may also happened if the mixture is boiled, due to the formation of silver oxide.

$\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{3}}\downarrow \xrightarrow{{}}\text{A}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{O}\downarrow +\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\uparrow $

concepts.title: Sepration of Basic radicals
concepts.conceptBody:
Basic Radicals

Separation of basic radicals into groups

For systematic analysis, the basic radicals have been classified into groups. Each group has a reagent, which shows the presence of the basic radical belonging to that particular group. It is necessary that the radical is completely removed in the respective group by using excess of group reagent, otherwise its presence creates difficulty in higher group.

It should be noted that:

1. Group I radicals $\left( \text{A}{{\text{g}}^{+}},\text{P}{{\text{b}}^{2+}},\text{Hg}_{2}^{2+} \right)$ are precipitated as chlorides because the solubility product of these chlorides $\left( \text{AgCl},\text{PbC}{{\text{l}}_{2}},\text{H}{{\text{g}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{l}}_{2}} \right)$ is less than the solubility products of chlorides of all other metal ions, which remain in solution.

2. Group II radicals are precipitated as sulphides because of their low solubility, whereas sulphide of other metals remain in solution because of their high solubility products. $\text{HCl}$ acts as a source of ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$, which decreases the concentration of ${{\text{S}}^{2-}}$ due to common ion effect. Hence, the decreased concentration of ${{\text{S}}^{2-}}$ is only sufficient to precipitate the Group II metals ions as sulphides.

3. Group III A radicals are precipitated as hydroxides and the $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$ suppresses the ionisation of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$ so that only the group III A cations are precipitated as hydroxide because of their low solubility product.

(i) Excess of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$ should not be added, else manganese will precipitate as $\text{Mn}{{\text{O}}_{2}}\cdot {{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

(ii) ${{\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)}_{2}}\text{S}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$ cannot be used in placed of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$ because the $\text{SO}_{4}^{2-}$ will give precipitate of $\text{BaS}{{\text{O}}_{4}}$.

(iii) $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{N}{{\text{O}}_{3}}$ cannot be used in place of $\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{Cl}$ as $\text{NO}_{3}^{-}$ions will oxidise $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}$ to $\text{M}{{\text{n}}^{3+}}$ and thus, $\text{Mn}{{(\text{OH})}_{3}}$ will be precipitated in III A group.

4. Ammonium hydroxide increases the ionisation of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ by removing ${{\text{H}}^{+}}$from ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ as unionised water.

${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}\rightleftharpoons 2{{\text{H}}^{+}}+{{\text{S}}^{2-}},\quad {{\text{H}}^{+}}+\text{O}{{\text{H}}^{-}}\xrightarrow{{}}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

Now the excess of ${{\text{S}}^{2-}}$ ions is available and hence the ionic product of group III B exceeds their solubility product and will be precipitated. In case ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$ is passed through a neutral solution, incomplete precipitation will take place due to the formation of $\text{HCl}$, which decreases the ionisation of ${{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}$. For example,

$\text{MnC}{{\text{l}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}~\text{S}\xrightarrow{{}}\text{MnS}+2\text{HCl}$
